Why are you interested in this role?: People analytics isn't just about pulling headcount reports—from my experience, it's about structuring data so the People team can actually trust it, ask better questions of it, and act on it. That's the part of the work I keep gravitating toward. At NextGen Healthcare I analyzed 2,000+ employee experience responses and turned them into reports the C-suite could actually decide on, and at ODN Oregon I worked with a team of consultants who partnered with an HR client to audit 300+ leave cases and quantify $362K in costs, refuting assumptions with evidence. This role—definitions, governance, self-service that holds up under scrutiny—is that instinct made into a job. It's the data foundation everything else on a People team depends on, and I genuinely like being the person who keeps it honest.
Why do you want to work at Faire?: What energizes me most is that this role sits right at the People-and-IT seam, building toward AI-augmented People tools. I genuinely believe in using data—and increasingly AI—to improve how processes and strategy decisions get made, rather than relying on assumptions, and that's exactly the direction this role is pointed. I lean on AI tools daily and have trained 600+ people on adopting new products and tools, so I'm comfortable translating between the people side and the technical side, which is what an AI roadmap built on cross-functional collaboration really needs. It's also just how I work: I treat people questions as systems to diagnose with data, not numbers to report out.
What excites you about this opportunity?: What excites me is the concrete work of making People data something the team can actually act on. A lot of People teams run on assumptions because the data isn't clean, consistently defined, or accessible enough to challenge them—and that's the specific gap I like closing. At InGenius Prep I do a version of this now: I interview stakeholders, turn their feedback into concrete data on where programs break down, and use it to improve communications, onboarding steps, and how our information is organized and tracked. Doing that at Faire's scale—clear definitions and reliable People data, then layering AI on top of a foundation that's actually solid—is the part I'd look forward to most. I care as much about the data being right as I do about the analysis on top of it.
Tell us about a time you used data to ch: At InGenius Prep, onboarding was inconsistent and program information was scattered, but the instinct was just to add more materials. Instead of guessing, I ran structured interviews with stakeholders and turned that feedback into concrete data on where people were actually getting stuck. The evidence pointed somewhere different than expected—the problem was fragmented communications and hard-to-find information, not missing content. So we focused on tightening communications and reorganizing and better tracking our digital libraries, which streamlined the onboarding process and cut onboarding time by 75%. It reinforced how I work: gather real data first, let it redirect the decision, then keep refining—exactly the discipline I'd bring to building People analytics here.
